topic: Someone discovers their neighbor is secretly wealthy
raw_output:
[story 1]

The manicured hedges of Mr. Henderson’s property always seemed to mock my own haphazard gardening. I watched from my kitchen window as he effortlessly guided a drone over his sprawling estate, a silent testament to the sheer excess he possessed. Every time he emerged, impeccably dressed, his tailored suit catching the sunlight, I felt a tightening in my chest. It was an irritating, simmering feeling, like watching a slow-motion train hurtle past a stationary object. I made sure my greetings were clipped, my smile minimal. "Morning, Mr. Henderson," I'd manage, my voice carefully neutral, devoid of any warmth. He would offer a practiced, distant nod, his eyes already scanning the horizon, as if I were merely an inconvenient piece of background scenery.

[story 2]

I found myself lingering by the shared fence line, ostensibly to retrieve a misplaced garden tool, but really just to observe. Mrs. Albright’s new speedboat, gleaming under the afternoon sun, was a beacon of ostentatious wealth that sliced through the quiet suburban air. A sharp, unpleasant prickle rose beneath my skin whenever I saw the size of her yacht. I tightened my grip on the rusty trowel until my knuckles turned white. I remember thinking, with a distinct sense of superiority, that her entire existence was a performance, a loud, glittering display designed solely to impress. I deliberately avoided eye contact, focusing instead on the chipped paint of the wooden slats separating our yards, wishing the very air around her felt somehow less expensive.

[story 3]

It started subtly: the impossible size of the delivery trucks that serviced the Henderson residence, the way their security detail moved with an almost military precision. I’d catch glimpses of their gleaming vehicles parked down the street, and a cold knot would form in my stomach. I remember one evening, peering through the blinds, watching a chauffeur discreetly unload crates from a black van. A bitter, sour taste flooded my mouth. It wasn't just that they were rich; it was the sheer, unearned magnitude of it, the way they seemed to occupy a different plane of reality than the rest of us. I slammed the window shut, the sound echoing too loudly in my small living room, a physical manifestation of my profound dissatisfaction with the disparity.
